God's command to present offerings "at its appointed time" reveals something crucial about worship that our age tends to overlook: holiness requires regularity, not just intensity. The daily lamb offerings at morning and twilight were not grand spectacles. They happened every single day, year after year, with precise measurements and careful attention to detail. Two lambs, one-tenth ephah of flour, one-quarter hin of oil, one-quarter hin of wine. The repetition matters. These weren't offerings born from sudden emotional fervor or personal inspiration. They were steadfast, disciplined acts of devotion woven into the fabric of ordinary time. The Israelites were learning that faithfulness to God is built on showing up—not when we feel moved, not when it's convenient, but at the appointed time.

What strikes the careful reader is that God calls these offerings "food for My food offerings" and repeatedly describes them as "a pleasing aroma to the LORD." This language can seem strange to modern ears, but it reveals God's desire for relationship. The smoke rising from the altar was meant to ascend continually, a visible expression of Israel's perpetual dependence on and allegiance to their God. Yet notice the progression through the chapter: daily offerings establish the foundation, Sabbath offerings sanctify time itself, monthly offerings mark the rhythm of creation, and the great feasts—Passover, Unleavened Bread, Weeks—commemorate what God has done. Each layer adds meaning. None replaces the others. Worship is not one-dimensional; it requires daily discipline, weekly remembrance, monthly acknowledgment, and annual celebration of God's mighty acts.

The presence of sin offerings woven throughout this chapter—a male goat appears at the Passover feast and the Feast of Weeks—shows that even in structured, obedient worship, the people needed atonement. Their offerings could not perfect their conscience or remove their guilt. They could express faith and dependence, but they could not accomplish what only Christ's blood would accomplish. For us who read this, these ancient commands point beyond themselves. We no longer bring lambs and bulls, yet the principle burns as fiercely as the altar fire: worship demands our time, our discipline, our resources, and our honest acknowledgment of need. And all of it finds its meaning in Christ, the unblemished Lamb whose offering makes the daily, weekly, monthly, and yearly repetitions of the Old Testament finally complete.
